scientific_scope: Derivation of the two-sector response kernel R_ij = C_0 delta_ij + C_2 I_ij from a radial momentum integral with sector A (scalar+longitudinal) and sector B (transverse) decomposition, including product-rule reorganization, projection, integration, limiting case, numerical verification, and physical interpretation.

nodes.scientific_scope: Definition of the two-point response kernel R_ij(k) as a rank-2 symmetric tensor in momentum space.
assumptions: Symmetric tensor: R_ij = R_ji
Momentum-space representation is well-defined
Einstein summation convention applies

nodes.scientific_scope: Definition of sector A kernel A_ij(k) = f(k) delta_ij + g(k) k_i k_j / k^2 and sector B kernel B_ij(k) = h(k) (delta_ij - k_i k_j / k^2).
assumptions: f(k), g(k), h(k) are real differentiable functions on (0, Lambda]
k^2 != 0 for all k > 0
Tensors obey standard flat-space index algebra

nodes.scientific_scope: Starting integral: R_ij = integral_0^Lambda dk/k [A_ij(k) + B_ij(k)] + O(epsilon^2).
assumptions: Integral converges for k in (0, Lambda]
O(epsilon^2) error estimate holds for the specified kernel functions
The measure dk/k is the correct radial measure in d = 2 + epsilon
caveats: The O(epsilon^2) bound assumes specific regularity of f, g, h at the boundaries

nodes.scientific_scope: Product rule identity: partial_k [F(k) G_ij(k)] = F'(k) G_ij(k) + F(k) partial_k G_ij(k).
assumptions: F(k) is differentiable on (0, Lambda]
G_ij(k) is differentiable on (0, Lambda]
Indices i, j are inert under partial_k in flat space
caveats: This is a standard calculus identity; verification is trivial

nodes.scientific_scope: Reorganized integrand after applying the product rule identity, separating total derivative and remainder contributions.
assumptions: Product rule applies term-by-term to the integrand
Boundary terms at k=0 and k=Lambda are treated separately
No integration by parts is used — only reorganization of the integrand structure
caveats: This is a formal reorganization; the equality holds pointwise in k

nodes.scientific_scope: Sector decomposition into R_ij^(A) and R_ij^(B) with exact reconstruction formula R_ij = R_ij^(A) + R_ij^(B).
assumptions: The decomposition A_ij + B_ij reconstructs the full kernel by construction
No cross-terms between sectors exist at the kernel level
The decomposition is exact before integration

nodes.scientific_scope: Projection of the sector-decomposed expression onto the isotropic (delta_ij) and anisotropic (I_ij) basis tensors.
assumptions: The basis {delta_ij, I_ij} spans the space of symmetric rank-2 tensors in d dimensions with a single preferred direction
Projection operators are orthogonal under the natural inner product A_ij B_ij
Normalization: I_ij I_ij = 1
caveats: The projection reduces tensorial equations to scalar coefficient equations

nodes.scientific_scope: Radial momentum integration of sector A (scalar + longitudinal contribution).
assumptions: Boundary terms at k = 0 vanish due to infrared regularity of f(k) and g(k)
Boundary terms at k = Lambda are retained as cutoff-dependent constants
Integration commutes with tensor projection operators
caveats: Exact analytic integration depends on the functional forms of f(k) and g(k)

nodes.scientific_scope: Radial momentum integration of sector B (transverse contribution).
assumptions: Boundary terms at k = 0 vanish due to infrared regularity of h(k)
Boundary terms at k = Lambda contribute to finite-cutoff corrections
Transverse projector k_i B_ij = 0 is preserved under integration
caveats: Exact analytic integration depends on the functional form of h(k)

nodes.scientific_scope: Limiting case epsilon -> 0: R_ij -> C_0^(0) delta_ij, with I_ij vanishing identically in d = 2.
assumptions: The limit epsilon -> 0+ is taken after integration
C_0^(0) is the finite limit of C_0 as epsilon -> 0
I_ij = 0 is an algebraic identity in d = 2 for the defined normalization

nodes.scientific_scope: Numerical verification of the integrated result against the 96-term expanded expression for sample parameter values.
assumptions: Sample values for f(k), g(k), h(k), epsilon, Lambda are representative
Numerical integration errors are controlled below O(epsilon^2, 1/Lambda)
The 96-term expression is the complete expansion of the starting integral after reorganization and decomposition
caveats: Numerical verification is not a proof; it supports but does not establish the analytic result

nodes.scientific_scope: Physical interpretation of C_0 as isotropic response coefficient, C_2 as anisotropic response coefficient, and the sector decomposition as compressible vs. incompressible mode separation.
assumptions: Linear response theory provides the physical context
The preferred direction n has a physical origin (e.g., external field or boundary)
Observable signatures are accessible via directional transport measurements
caveats: Physical interpretation is model-dependent; alternative interpretations may exist

assumption_scope: Decomposition is exact: A_ij + B_ij reconstructs full kernel
{delta_ij, I_ij} is a complete basis for symmetric rank-2 tensors
edges.projection_scope: Contraction with delta_ij and I_ij to extract scalar coefficients C_0 and C_2

assumption_scope: Integration over k from 0 to Lambda
Integration over k from 0 to Lambda
R_ij = R_ij^(A) + R_ij^(B) reconstruction holds after integration

edges.projection_scope: Numerical evaluation of 96-term expression vs. 2-term result
caveats: Numerical support is not a proof

assumption_scope: epsilon -> 0+ limit commutes with integration (uniform convergence assumed)

caveats: Interpretation is self-referential in this synthetic fixture

assumption_scope: F and G are differentiable on (0, Lambda]
Partial_k commutes with tensor indices in flat space
